title: Living in Boulder vlog 2 -  7 -  2017: Google Earthing Amphitheater of Casestres in Rome

enrichment.summary: Kyle records an early morning vlog from Boulder discussing his ongoing Google Earth project recreating a Roman amphitheater (modeled after the Colosseum, sized for a Roman legion of 4,800 people), his interest in generational cycle theory (Strauss-Howe fourth turning), and plans to apply that framework to Roman history back to 750 BC. He also mentions exploring Google Earth Moon and Mars and watching space-related YouTube videos.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Finds it fascinating and believes it is a powerful lens for understanding history
Believes Rome has enough recorded history to map the Strauss-Howe cycle all the way back to 750 BC, and wants to do this
Thinks recreating Apollo buggy routes and space missions on Google Earth would make fun YouTube videos

entities: Alexis de Tocqueville (implied, mentioned as subject of a book)

claims.claim: I want to add more of my own to make it 4-800 people because that's a Roman legion.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I designed something looking like a costume, which is a mini-calls room, half the circumference of the original.

claims.subject: Kyle

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Generational cycles are like my alley because I had them from the beginning of Rome on my timeline.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The four cycles are artist, nomad, hero, and profit, each with different personalities.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I copied all the descriptions of the different generations from the Arthurian generation to now.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The generations are programmed back to the 1650s, which is almost a quarter of the way to the beginning of the Roman Empire.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I think it's possible to do a generational breakdown from the beginning of Rome.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Rome is the only place with enough history throughout its entire existence to do a generational breakdown.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Rome goes back to 750 BC, which is something I really want to study.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Looking at history through generational cycles is a perfect way to understand it.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I was watching YouTube videos about space, including a channel called Vintage Space.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I looked on Google Earth Moon and saw the space missions' buggy routes in 3D.

claims.subject: Kyle

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I saw a rocket that took off from Earth and was in space within a couple of minutes.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I built a little submarine to see how far below the water they were.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I can attach anything to my bike wing and do anything with it.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm going to focus on the generational thing tomorrow.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to do a test for the 20th century to see how good I am at 20th-century history.
